title: You Can Have Your Ashes Turned Into a Playable Vinyl Record, When Your Day Comes
description:
in Life, Music |   	May 4th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Even in death we are only lim┬şit┬şed by our imag┬şi┬şna┬ştion in how we want to go out. The
body:
in Life, Music |   	May 4th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Even in death we are only lim┬şit┬şed by our imag┬şi┬şna┬ştion in how we want to go out. There are now ways to turn our corpse into a tree, or have our ash┬şes shot into space, or press our ash┬şes into dia┬şmondsÔÇôI believe Super┬şman is involved in that last one. And now for the music lover, a com┬şpa┬şny called And Viny┬şly will press your ash┬şes into a playable vinyl record.
You like that pun┬şny com┬şpa┬şny name? ThereÔÇÖs more: the busi┬şness lets the dear depart┬şed ÔÇťLive on from beyond the groove.ÔÇŁ Hear that groan? ThatÔÇÖs the deceased lit┬şer┬şal┬şly spin┬şning in their graveÔÇŽon a turntable.
The UK-based com┬şpa┬şny has been around since 2009, when Jason Leach launched it ÔÇťjust for funÔÇŁ at first. But a lot of peo┬şple liked the idea and have kept him in busi┬şness.
It will cost, how┬şev┬şer. The basic ser┬şvice gen┬şer┬şal┬şly costs between ┬ú1000 and ┬ú3000 GBP, and it part┬şly depends on how many vinyl records you pro┬şduce. From what we can tell, you can┬şnot use copy┬şright-pro┬ştect┬şed music to fill up the 18ÔÇô22 min┬şutes per side. So no ÔÇťFree BirdÔÇŁ or ÔÇťWe Are the Cham┬şpi┬şons,ÔÇŁ unfor┬ştu┬şnate┬şly. But you can put any┬şthing else: a voice record┬şing, or the sounds of nature, or com┬şplete silence. Get more infor┬şma┬ştion over at the com┬şpa┬şnyÔÇÖs FAQ.
No doubt, the ser┬şvice can pro┬şvide com┬şfort and a mem┬şo┬şry trig┬şger for those left behind. The above video, ÔÇťHear┬şing Madge,ÔÇŁ is a short doc about a son who took record┬şings of his moth┬şer and used And Viny┬şly to make a record out of them. ItÔÇÖs sweet.
ÔÇťIÔÇÖm sure a lot of peo┬şple think that itÔÇÖs creepy, a lot of peo┬şple think itÔÇÖs sac┬şri┬şle┬şgious,ÔÇŁ the man says. ÔÇťBut I know my moth┬şer wouldnÔÇÖt have. She wouldÔÇÖve thought it was a hoot.ÔÇŁ
Jason Leach, a musi┬şcian and vinyl col┬şlec┬ştor him┬şself, talks of the imme┬şdi┬şa┬şcy of sound and what it means to many.
ÔÇťSound is vibrat┬şing you, the room, and itÔÇÖs actu┬şal┬şly mov┬şing the air around you,ÔÇŁ he says. ÔÇťAnd thatÔÇÖs whatÔÇÖs so pow┬şer┬şful about hear┬şing someoneÔÇÖs voice on a record. TheyÔÇÖre actu┬şal┬şly mov┬şing the air and for me thatÔÇÖs pow┬şer┬şful.ÔÇŁ
Note: An ear┬şli┬şer ver┬şsion of this post appeared on our site in 2017.
Relat┬şed Con┬ştent:
John CleeseÔÇÖs Eulo┬şgy for Gra┬şham Chap┬şman: ÔÇśGood Rid┬şdance, the Free-Load┬şing Bas┬ştard, I Hope He FriesÔÇÖ
Bronze Age Britons Turned Bones of Dead Rel┬şa┬ştives into Musi┬şcal Instru┬şments & Orna┬şments
Watch Carl SaganÔÇÖs ÔÇťA Glo┬şri┬şous DawnÔÇŁ Become the First Vinyl Record Played in Space, Cour┬ştesy of Jack White
Death: A Free Online Phi┬şlos┬şo┬şphy Course from Yale Helps You Grap┬şple with the Inescapable
 Ted Mills is a free┬şlance writer on the arts.

schema.headline: You Can Have Your Ashes Turned Into a Playable Vinyl Record, When Your Day Comes

title: Chuck JonesÔÇÖ The Dot and the Line Celebrates Geometry & Hard Work: An Oscar-Winning Animation (1965)
description:
in Animation, Education, Film, Math |   	May 4th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

The ani┬şmat┬şed short above, The Dot and the Line, direct┬şed by the
body:
in Animation, Education, Film, Math |   	May 4th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

The ani┬şmat┬şed short above, The Dot and the Line, direct┬şed by the great Chuck Jones and nar┬şrat┬şed by Eng┬şlish actor Robert Mor┬şley, won an Oscar in 19656 for Best Ani┬şmat┬şed Short Film. Based on a book writ┬şten┬áby Nor┬şton Juster,┬áÔÇťThe Dot and the LineÔÇŁ tells the sto┬şry of a romance between two geo┬şmet┬şric shapesÔÇötaking the arche┬ştyp┬şal nar┬şra┬ştive tra┬şjec┬şto┬şry of boy meets girl, los┬şes girl, wins girl in the end (find┬şing him┬şself along the way) and inject┬şing it with some fas┬şci┬şnat┬şing social com┬şmen┬ştary that still res┬şonates almost fifty years lat┬şer. One way of watch┬şing ÔÇťThe Dot and the LineÔÇŁ is as a ÔÇťtri┬şumph of the nerdÔÇŁ sto┬şry, where an anx┬şious square (as in ÔÇťuncoolÔÇŁ) Line has to com┬şpete with a hip┬şster beat┬şnik Squig┬şgle of a rival for the affec┬ştions of a flighty Dot.
The Line begins the film ÔÇťstiff as a stickÔÇŽ dull, con┬şven┬ştion┬şal and repressedÔÇŁ (as his love inter┬şest says of him) in con┬ştrast to the groovy Squig┬şgle and his groovy bebop sound┬ştrack. With the pos┬şsi┬şble sug┬şges┬ştion that this love trans┬şgress┬şes mid-cen┬ştu┬şry racial bound┬şaries, the LineÔÇÖs friends dis┬şap┬şprove and tell him to give it up, since ÔÇťthey all look alike any┬şway.ÔÇŁ But the Line per┬şsists in his fol┬şly, indulging in some Wal┬şter Mit┬şty-like rever┬şies of hero┬şic endeav┬şors that might win over his Dot. Final┬şly, using ÔÇťgreat self-con┬ştrol,ÔÇŁ he man┬şages to bend him┬şself into an angle, then anoth┬şer, then a series of sim┬şple, then very com┬şplex, shapes, becom┬şing, we might assume, some kind of math┬şe┬şmat┬şi┬şcal wiz. After refin┬şing his tal┬şents alone, he goes off to show them to Dot, who is ÔÇťover┬şwhelmedÔÇŁ and delight┬şed and who ÔÇťgig┬şgles like a school┬şgirl.ÔÇŁ
Here the sub┬ştext of the nerd-gets-the-girl sto┬şry┬şline man┬şi┬şfests a fair┬şly con┬şser┬şv┬şa┬ştive cri┬ştique of the ÔÇťanar┬şchyÔÇŁ of the Squig┬şgle, whom the Dot comes to see as ÔÇťundis┬şci┬şplined, grace┬şless, coarseÔÇŁ and oth┬şer unflat┬şter┬şing adjec┬ştives while the lineÔÇöwho pro┬şclaimed to him┬şself ear┬şli┬şer that ÔÇťfree┬şdom is not a license for chaosÔÇŁÔÇöis ÔÇťdaz┬şzling, clever, mys┬şte┬şri┬şous, ver┬şsa┬ştile, light, elo┬şquent, pro┬şfound, enig┬şmat┬şic, com┬şplex, and com┬şpelling.ÔÇŁ I can almost imag┬şine that George Will had a hand in the writ┬şing, which is to say that itÔÇÖs enor┬şmous┬şly clever, and enor┬şmous┬şly invest┬şed in the val┬şues of self-con┬ştrol, hard work, and dis┬şci┬şpline, and dis┬ştrust┬şful of spon┬ştane┬şity, free play, and gen┬şer┬şal groovi┬şness. At the end of the film, our Dot and Line go off to live ÔÇťif not hap┬şpi┬şly ever after, at least rea┬şson┬şably soÔÇŁ in some cozy sub┬şurb, no doubt. The moral of the sto┬şry? ÔÇťTo the vec┬ştor belong the spoils.ÔÇŁ
If you would like to sup┬şport the mis┬şsion of Open Cul┬şture, con┬şsid┬şer mak┬şing┬áa dona┬ştion to our site. ItÔÇÖs hard to rely 100% on ads, and your con┬ştri┬şbu┬ştions will help us con┬ştin┬şue pro┬şvid┬şing the best free cul┬ştur┬şal and edu┬şca┬ştion┬şal mate┬şri┬şals to learn┬şers every┬şwhere. You can con┬ştribute through Pay┬şPal, Patre┬şon, and Ven┬şmo (@openculture). Thanks!
Relat┬şed Con┬ştent┬á
Carl Sagan Explains Evo┬şlu┬ştion in an 8ÔÇĹMinute Ani┬şma┬ştion
Watch ÔÇťGeom┬şe┬ştry of Cir┬şcles,ÔÇŁ the Abstract┬áSesame Street┬áAni┬şma┬ştion Scored by Philip Glass (1979)
Jour┬şney to the Cen┬şter of a Tri┬şan┬şgle: Watch the 1977 Dig┬şi┬ştal Ani┬şma┬ştion That Demys┬şti┬şfies Geom┬şe┬ştry
Josh Jones is a writer and musi┬şcian based in Durham, NC.┬á

title: Artists, Read the Fine Print
description:
Daily Newsletter
                The insidiousness of artists' contacts, visitors will now judge the Venice Biennale, and TrumpÔÇÖ
body:
Daily Newsletter
                The insidiousness of artists' contacts, visitors will now judge the Venice Biennale, and TrumpÔÇÖs border crew damages 1,000-year-old Native etching. 
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
        
            ÔÇťThe art world runs on informality,ÔÇŁ artist Damien Davis writes, ÔÇťuntil informality stops serving the institution.ÔÇŁ In todayÔÇÖs must-read piece, Davis takes on the many ways so-called ÔÇťstandardÔÇŁ contracts actually undermine artistsÔÇÖ power. The cost of pushing back feels higher than the risk of signing, he writes, and sometimes, you canÔÇÖt afford to walk away.Speaking of walking away: The latest twist in this yearÔÇÖs Venice Biennale, after the awards jury resigned last week, is that you are now its judge. There will be no Golden Lions, the eventÔÇÖs top prize; instead, ticket holders will vote on ÔÇťVisitor Lions,ÔÇŁ with awards announced when the show closes in November. Conveniently, Israel and Russia are eligible after the jury said it would ban them from consideration due to charges of crimes against humanity. Okay, little lion, letÔÇÖs hear that roar.┬áÔÇö Lisa Yin Zhang, associate editor
Artists, read carefully before you sign. (illustration by Shari Flores/Hyperallergic) What Artists Sign AwayLong consignment periods, moral rights waivers, and opaque ÔÇťstandardÔÇŁ contracts serve the institution more than the artist. | Damien Davis
                    
                    
                    
                        
                            SMFA at Tufts Presents Passages, the 2026 MFA Thesis ExhibitionOn view from May 5 to 17 in Medford, Massachusetts, this exhibition represents the graduating class and their journeys through worlds visited and imaged.
                        
                        
                            Learn more
                        
                    
            NewsThe Golden Lion at the 59th Art Biennale (photo Felix H├Ârhager/picture alliance via Getty Images)Venice Biennale scrapped the "Golden Lion" awards, stating that the public will vote on ÔÇťVisitor LionsÔÇŁ instead. Construction crews building President TrumpÔÇÖs hostile border wall damaged a portion of a Native American archeological site in Arizona estimated to be at least 1,000 years old.From Our CriticsDiedrick Brackens, ÔÇťBlood CompassÔÇŁ(2023) (photo Natasha Boas/Hyperallergic)Diedrick BrackensÔÇÖs Tapestries Beckon the Light of FreedomIn this Bay Area artistÔÇÖs hands, weaving becomes a site of experimentation and refusal. | Natasha Boas 
                    
                    
                    
                        
                            Rising Up: Rocky and the Making of MonumentsFeaturing works from antiquity to today, the exhibition at the Philadelphia Museum of Art explores the Rocky statue and its impact on the cityÔÇÖs culture, community, and public art.
                        
                        
                            Learn more
                        
                    
            Guide Flora Rebollo, ÔÇťFriedÔÇŁ (2026) (photo Valentina Di Liscia/Hyperallergic)15 Art Shows to See in NYC This MayLynette Yiadom-Boakye captures quietude, Seydou Ke├»ta documents a revolution, Ren├ęe Green compiles an autoethnography, and much more. | Hrag Vartanian,┬áValentina Di Liscia,┬áLisa Yin Zhang,┬áJohn Yau,┬áJasmine Weber,┬áIsa FarfanView of Gerardo Camargo's studio (photo Gerardo Camargo)A View From the Easel with Gerardo CamargoÔÇťI like to play with materials that have a history of manipulation.ÔÇŁDon Dugal on Hakan TopalÔÇÖs ÔÇťThe Death of the Art SchoolÔÇŁ:What a pithy and accurate analysis of the subversive results of arts administration of the last 40 years! Experience, integrity, insight and collegiality have been denigrated in favor of those skilled in bean-counting, CV stuffing and aggressive behavior. It is now possible that highly-paid arts administrators and jurors have no serious first hand experience in art manufacture.Podcast From the Archive ÔÇťThe Invisible Enemy Should Not ExistÔÇŁ by Michael Rakowitz in LondonÔÇÖs Trafalgar SquareMichael Rakowitz Discusses Withdrawing from the 2019 Whitney Biennial, and His Leonard Cohen ProblemThe artist shares his thoughts on museums, power, art, and ideology. | Hrag VartanianOpportunities This MonthResidencies, fellowships, grants, and open calls from the Bennett Prize, Oak Spring Garden Foundation, and more in our May 2026 list of opportunities for artists, writers, and art workers.
                  
                      
                        Daily Newsletter
                      
                      
                        Newsletter

title: L├ęvy Gorvy Dayan Bets on Urgency With New LGD Hammer Sales Platform
description: L├ęvy Gorvy Dayan is rolling out a new way to sell high-value art, though gallery cofounder Brett Gorvy is quick to lower the temperature on the idea
body:
L├ęvy Gorvy Dayan is rolling out a new way to sell high-value art, though gallery cofounder Brett Gorvy is quick to lower the temperature on the idea. The galleryÔÇÖs new platform, LGD Hammer, isnÔÇÖt meant to remake the market. ÔÇťWe arenÔÇÖt inventing a new paradigm, but it┬áis┬áa development,ÔÇŁ he said.

	The platform, he said is informed by recent changed in the market. Private sales have slowed. Collectors want time to look, think, and negotiate. Deals stretch out. ÔÇťThereÔÇÖs no urgency,ÔÇŁ Gorvy said. Even strong works can sit while buyers circle.
	

	
		Related Articles
	


	Auctions still do the opposite. They create a moment: you either show up and bid, or you donÔÇÖt. That pressure matters more right now because the market has spent the past couple of years resetting. Auction houses have leaned on lower estimates to get works moving, and the results have been solid. Strong sell-through rates and a run of clean sales have pulled sellers back toward competition.

	Now, LGD Hammer wants to bring that pressure into a gallery setting. Instead of sending a work into a packed evening sale, the gallery will offer one painting at a set time to a smaller group of buyers. Dominique L├ęvy, a cofounder of the New YorkÔÇôbased gallery, will serve as auctioneer, drawing on her years at ChristieÔÇÖs to run the sale.

	The first work up is Willem de KooningÔÇÖs┬áMilkmaid┬á(1984), carrying an estimate of $10 million to $15 million. The choice says a lot about where the market is, since collectors have drifted back to artists with long track records and deep institutional support. De Kooning fits that bill, Gorvy said, and his market has held up across multiple regions, with steady demand from Asia. The price range matters too, since works in the $10 millionÔÇô$20 million band are still moving.

	The work will be presented more like an exhibition than a lot in a sale. Milkmaid will be on view in a stand-alone presentation at the galleryÔÇÖs Upper East Side space, but only by appointment from May 2 through the sale on May 16.┬áÔÇťWeÔÇÖre not in 2021, when people were buying things unseen,ÔÇŁ Gorvy said.

	The sale itself will take place on May 16, with bidding conducted by phone while participants follow along online, watching both the L├ęvy and the de Kooning in real time. Bidders, of course, will remain anonymous.
	

	Gorvy was careful not to frame the platform as a direct response to platforms like Fair Warning, which also offers one work for bidding at a time. LGD Hammer is built around the slower experience of seeing a work in a gallery, Gorvy said, with context and time to consider it, before the pressure of bidding sets in.

	Buyers, for their part, are more careful than they were a few years ago. They want to see the work in person, and they want context. They want to feel comfortable with the number. But they still respond to competition, which is one thing LGD Hammer is hoping to tap.

schema.headline: L├ęvy Gorvy Dayan Bets on Urgency With New LGD Hammer Sales Platform

title: A $35 M. Warhol, a $45 M. Basquiat, and More: WhoÔÇÖs Selling The Top Works in the May Sales?
description: ItÔÇÖs springtime in the art world, and that can mean only one thing. Not the Venice Biennale, though that is no doubt where many dealers, collectors,
body:
ItÔÇÖs springtime in the art world, and that can mean only one thing. Not the Venice Biennale, though that is no doubt where many dealers, collectors, and artists are this week. No, weÔÇÖre talking about the May marquee sales.

	This yearÔÇÖs sales arrive, as always, at something of an inflection point for the market. Following two years of sales declines, the global art market returned to modest growth of 4 percent last year, according to the most recent Art Basel & UBS Art Market Report. The total sales figure of $59.6 billion was buoyed not by collectors piling back into contemporary art but by a surge of high-quality consignments in the November sales, namely that of Leonard LauderÔÇÖs collection at SothebyÔÇÖs, which was headlined by a $236.4 million Klimt.
	

	
		Related Articles
	


	While there are no works in the spring sales season expected to approach that eye-watering result, that doesnÔÇÖt mean there arenÔÇÖt trophy works. The major houses seem to have received the message: Collectors want artists with deep, stable markets and artworks with established, top-shelf provenance. Thankfully, for them, works from some of the most storied collections have shaken loose. 

	ChristieÔÇÖs will be offering 16 lots from the estate of S. I. Newhouse, topped by a Constantin Br├óncu╚Öi sculpture, Dana├»de (ca. 1913), and a Jackson Pollock painting, Number 7A, 1948, which are estimated at $100 million each. The house is also offering three lots from the collection of former Museum of Modern Art board president Agnes Gund, the most expensive of which is a 1964 Mark Rothko work No. 15 (Two Greens and Red Stripe), estimated at $80 million. ChristieÔÇÖs rounds out its evening sales with a group of Gerhard Richter paintings from the collection of Marian Goodman, who died in January. The top piece in that grouping carries a $50 million estimate.

	Not to be outdone, SothebyÔÇÖs has its own Rothko trophy work on offer, the 1957 painting Brown and Blacks in Reds, estimated at $100 million. That work is one of 24 on offer from the collection of the late dealer and financier Robert Mnuchin. There is also a second Rothko, No. 1 (1949), estimated at $15 million to $20 million. SothebyÔÇÖs is also offering some 50 works from the collection of David and Shoshanna Wingate, including the Giacometti sculpture La Clairi├Ęre (Composition avec neuf figures), estimated between $18 million and $25 million.
	

	Those are all the big-name consignors announced, and touted, by the auction houses. What about the unnamed consignors for other top lots in the sales? Well, ARTnews can reveal more than a few. (Note: As always, none of the auction houses will confirm or deny any consignors.)

	At the top of the pile is Jean-Michel BasquiatÔÇÖs monumental 1983 painting Museum Security (Broadway Meltdown), set for SothebyÔÇÖs contemporary art evening sale on May 14, with an estimate of $45 million. Several sources told ARTnews that the consignor is, in fact, John Sayegh-Belchatowski. The painting last sold to an unnamed buyer for $14.6 million at ChristieÔÇÖs London in 2013; at the time, the Baer Faxt reported that Sayegh-Belchatowski was the buyer. And while the collector did not respond to a request for comment, he posted about the work on Instagram shortly after the consignment was announced. His Instagram profile picture is also of the work, from when it was on view at SwitzerlandÔÇÖs Fondation Beyeler.

	Also in that SothebyÔÇÖs sale is Elizabeth PeytonÔÇÖs 1996 painting EarlÔÇÖs Court (Liam + Noel), carrying an estimate of $1.5 million to $2 million. That work appears to be from the collection of New YorkÔÇôbased neurosurgeon and collector Frank Moore and his late wife Nina, who appeared on the 2025 edition of the ARTnews Top 200 list. Per SothebyÔÇÖs, the work was last sold in 1998; a catalogue for the 2008 New Museum exhibition ÔÇťLive Forever: Elizabeth PeytonÔÇŁ lists the Moores as the lenders of that work.
	

	One last reveal for that SothebyÔÇÖs sale: Agnes MartinÔÇÖs 1981 work Untitled #10, which carries a $7 million to $10 million estimate, was sold to its current owner in 1998 by Sperone Westwater. Per Tiffany BellÔÇÖs catalogue raisonn├ę for the artist, first published in 2017, the work is owned by Richard I. Furman. 


			
			
			
	
			
					Andy WarholÔÇÖs 1963 work ÔÇťDouble┬áElvis┬á[Ferus Type]ÔÇŁ carries a $25 millionÔÇô$35 million estimate in ChristieÔÇÖs New YorkÔÇÖs 20th century evening sale in May.
									Courtesy ChristieÔÇÖs
	



	One of the top lots in ChristieÔÇÖs 20th century evening sale on May 18, Andy WarholÔÇÖs 1963 work Double Elvis [Ferus Type] has a considerably longer history. Passing through no fewer than a dozen hands in its 60-plus years in existence, including Leo Castelli and Ileana Sonnabend, the work was most famously on view in Las VegasÔÇÖs Palms Casino Resort, as part of an art-focused revamp of the Old Vegas icon after its purchase by Station Casinos for $313 million. Brothers Frank J. Fertitta III and Lorenzo Fertitta, the CEO and president and the chairman, respectively, of Station, are noted art collectors, with an emphasis on American Pop art and Abstract Expressionism. Several sources confirmed to ARTnews that the Fertittas are the consignors of Double Elvis.

	In ChristieÔÇÖs day sales are several works that appear to be consigned by ARTnews Top 200 collector Elie Khouri. First is the 2010 Lynette Yiadom-Boakye painting Shoot the Desperate, Hug the Needy, estimated at $700,000 to $1 million. That work was originally bought by movie mogul Michael Lynne and his wife Ninah from New York dealer Jack Shainman in 2010. However, at some point he returned it to Shainman, who then sold it to Khouri in 2018. The Dubai-based collector proudly posted it to Instagram in 2019, and last year the painting was shown hanging in his home in an interview in Frieze magazine. (Khouri declined to comment.)
	

	The Yiadom-Boakye work is listed as ÔÇťProperty from a Distinguished CollectionÔÇŁ; other works in that sale carrying the same designation are Richard PrinceÔÇÖs 2005 painting My Mother-in-Law, estimated at $120,000 to $180,000, which was last purchased at ChristieÔÇÖs London in 2017, and a 2021 mixed-media piece by Alex Da Corte, The Sexy Alphabet Dream, estimated at $70,000 to $100,000.

	And if you are wondering who is behind the mystery collection carrying the appellation ÔÇťA Matter of Seeing: Property from a Distinguished CollectionÔÇŁ in ChristieÔÇÖs postwar and contemporary art day sale, ARTnews has heard from a reliable source that it is none other than Ronald Lauder. All 18 works from Lauder, according to auction houseÔÇÖs provenance records, were originally owned by the Est├ęe Lauder Company. The works are mostly works on paper and some screen prints, including an untitled Brice Marden work made between 1965 and 1967, estimated at $200,000 to $300,000; a graphite seascape by Vija Celmins from 1972, with an estimate of $1.5 million to $2 million; and a deep red canvas covered in teal spots by Larry Poons, painted in 1965, estimated at $400,000 to $600,000.

	Lastly, Phillips may be trying to catch some of the buzz from SothebyÔÇÖs Mnuchin sale by designating its untitled 1948 Pollock painting as ÔÇťProperty Formerly from the Collection of Robert Mnuchin.ÔÇŁ The work was indeed in the late dealerÔÇÖs collection from 1990 until November 2024, when it came up for sale at Phillips. It failed to sell. Then things got complicated when the third-party guarantor, David Mimran, son of billionaire Jean-Claude Mimran, allegedly failed to pony up the funds; Phillips is currently suing Mimran. The painting now carries an estimate of $7 million to $10 million.

schema.headline: A $35 M. Warhol, a $45 M. Basquiat, and More: WhoÔÇÖs Selling The Top Works in the May Sales?

title: Derrick Adams to Install Monumental Portrait of Koyo Kouoh in Venice During the Biennale
description: The untimely passing of curator Koyo Kouoh, the artistic director of the 2026 Venice Biennale, has been deeply felt throughout the art world for the
body:
The untimely passing of curator Koyo Kouoh, the artistic director of the 2026 Venice Biennale, has been deeply felt throughout the art world for the past year. Now, artist Derrick Adams will pay tribute to Kouoh via a monumental installation in Venice this week.

	A banner version of his collage Heavy is the head that wears the crown┬á(2026) will be installed on the facade of the Palazzetto dello sport Giobatta Gianquinto,┬áfacing the Rio della Tana, near the Arsenale, one of the BiennaleÔÇÖs main venues. Done in his signature style, this newly realized work features a portrait of Kouoh, whose face and arms are rendered as different geometrical shapes; above her head is the word ÔÇťJOY,ÔÇŁ from which golden rays radiate.
	

	
		Related Articles
	


	ÔÇťMy hope that this important contribution acts as a reminder that the ongoing fight for what is right requires most of our attention, and we make space to give those we respect┬átheir flowers┬áfor their commitment to the creative community,ÔÇŁ Adams told ARTnews in an emailed interview.

	The idea for creating this tribute came from Francesco Bonami, who is curating the presentation. Bonami had first met Kouoh when he invited her to sit on the Golden Lion jury for his 2003 Biennale. They never formally collaborated again, but stayed in touch and he followed her career over the next two decades.

	ÔÇťThe week before her appointment, I had a feeling she would be chosen, so I reached out to encourage her,ÔÇŁ Bonami said in an email to ARTnews. ÔÇťWe promised to meet and exchange notes about my experience in Venice and her plans for the future, but unfortunately, that never happened.ÔÇŁ

	After KouohÔÇÖs passing, Bonami conducted a studio visit with Adams, who was working ÔÇťon a series of drawings dedicated to major Black figures in art, culture, politics, and music who had passed away,ÔÇŁ Bonami said. ÔÇťI suggested that we create something in Venice as a tribute to Koyo. Although Derrick had only met her briefly, we both revisited interviews where she spoke about celebrating joy in relation to Black painting and art. From there, the project developed almost as an improvised intervention.ÔÇŁ

	Adams said his work is motivated by the desire to ÔÇťcelebrate and highlight those devoted to uplifting┬áothers, ÔÇŽ especially at times when tragedy overshadows major accomplishments. From my observation, Koyo Kouoh did a great┬áthing and I wanted as many people walking the streets of Venice for the Biennale, including the locals to take notice.ÔÇŁ
	

	The decision to place it on the facade of a building, as a public mural, was to make it ÔÇťsomething accessible to everyone, outside the exclusive networks of parties and events that happen during the Biennale,ÔÇŁ Bonami said. Added Adams, ÔÇťI wanted my contribution┬áto be meaningful.ÔÇŁ

	The Biennale begins its professional previews this week, ahead of its public opening on May 9, and the art world will soon get a chance to see KouohÔÇÖs final exhibition. Her untimely passing, Bonami said, ÔÇťis a tragic and unprecedented turn of events. Yet, she had a spirit and strength that seem to endure beyond her physical presence. When ideas are powerful, they survive beyond the limits and tragedies of life. I hope people will feel that continuity in her exhibition.ÔÇŁ

	For his part, Adams connected his portrait of Kouoh to the larger concerns of the world, which her exhibition will almost certainly touch upon. ÔÇťWe should always remind ourselves that despite the many challenges we face, even during these times of political turmoil or internal strife,ÔÇŁ Adams said of the work, ÔÇťit┬áis also important to celebrate loudly the people in our worldwide community who have the vision to see beyond the obstacles before them and show up for those without a voice.ÔÇŁ

schema.headline: Derrick Adams to Install Monumental Portrait of Koyo Kouoh in Venice During the Biennale

title: Sellers at the May Marquee Auctions Revealed, Bogot├íÔÇÖs MAMBO Museum Loses Its Director, and More: Morning Links for May 4, 2026
description: MAY MARQUEE MASS. The spring auction season is an upon us, and ARTnews has done some sleuthing into whoÔÇÖs selling what. For one, thereÔÇÖs Jean-Michel
body:
MAY MARQUEE MASS. The spring auction season is an upon us, and ARTnews has done some sleuthing into whoÔÇÖs selling what. For one, thereÔÇÖs Jean-Michel BasquiatÔÇÖs monumental panting Museum Security (Broadway Meltdown), from 1983, set for SothebyÔÇÖs contemporary art sale on May 14, estimated at $45 million. According to sources, the consignor is Joahn Sayegh-Belchatowski. And if youÔÇÖre wondering whoÔÇÖs behind the mystery collection dubbed ÔÇťA Matter of Seeing: Property from a Distinguished CollectionÔÇŁ in a ChristieÔÇÖs postwar and contemporary art day sale, that would be none other than Ronald Lauder. Meanwhile, in other auction news, ARTnews reports that L├ęvy Gorvy Dayan is betting on a new auction-gallery hybrid selling model, in the hopes of bringing new energy to a slower-paced primary market, where deals stretch out over time. ÔÇťThereÔÇÖs no urgency,ÔÇŁ Brett Gorvy said. Lastly, as the Art Newspaper reported last week, British billionaire Joe Lewis is returning to SothebyÔÇÖs in June to sell another $200 million in art from his collection, following a $48 million sale in March.
	

	
		Related Articles
	


	ORTIZ OUSTED FROM MAMBO. Martha Ortiz has left the Museo de Arte Moderno de Bogot├í following accusations that she harassed staff and created a toxic workplace environment, reports the Art Newspaper. Her departure comes following the unexpected dismissal of the Colombian museumÔÇÖs artistic director, Eugenio Viola, in February. In a statement, the museum said Ortiz was retiring, and that the board was on the hunt for new leadership. She became the director of the museum in 2024 with no experience in the field, having worked previously in journalism and media. At the time of ViolaÔÇÖs February departure, after seven years as chief curator and artistic director, he stated that his exit ÔÇťfollowed my decision to raise concerns with the board in September 2025 regarding the progressive deterioration of working conditions.ÔÇŁ An open letter by art workers followed, asking for explanations regarding his firing.

	Digest:

	Artist Derrick Adams will install a monumental portrait of Koyo Kouoh near the Arsenale in Venice. Kouoh, the artistic director of the 2026 Venice Biennale, died a year ago, at 58. [ARTnews]
	

	The cartoonist Nicole Hollander, who wrote and illustrated the ÔÇťSylviaÔÇŁ comic strip about an acerbic feminist, has died at 86. [The New York Times]

	On Friday, adventurous art lovers were offered free entry to the Fondation BeyelerÔÇÖs blockbuster Paul C├ęzanne exhibition in Basel, Switzerland, if they showed up in their swimming suits, courtesy ofÔÇöyou guessed itÔÇöthe artist Maurizio Cattelan, who conceived of the idea. [France 24 and AFP]

	Gagosian gallery in London is going to create Air Package on a Ceiling, a previously unrealized installation that Christo designed in 1968. ItÔÇÖs been described as a kind of internally lit ÔÇťcloud.ÔÇŁ [The Guardian]

	Kicker:

	AMARO VITA. When it comes to arts and culture, the right-wing Italian Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni appears to have taken a page from President TrumpÔÇÖs book on political interference. The outcome, familiar to Americans, is far from la dolce vita. The Financial Times has an update on ItalyÔÇÖs ÔÇťculture wars,ÔÇŁ which have been plaguing the country as the Venice Biennale readies for pre-openings this week. Since her election in 2022, Meloni has tried to reshape the countryÔÇÖs arts and cultural institutions by appointing a host of new, politically conservative leaders. The Italian government is in ÔÇťa fight to appoint their own peopleÔÇöto show that they have good right-wing intellectuals or artists,ÔÇŁ explained professor Andrea Mammone. Yet these political appointees have often had little, or no expertise in the given fields they were meant to lead, resulting in all kinds of turmoil. Just yesterday, Italian culture minister Alessandro Giuli lashed out at the Venice Biennale director Pietrangelo Buttafuoco, saying the latter had ÔÇťfallen victim to a pacifist fantasy,ÔÇŁ by including the Russian pavilion.

schema.headline: Sellers at the May Marquee Auctions Revealed, Bogot├íÔÇÖs MAMBO Museum Loses Its Director, and More: Morning Links for May 4, 2026
